title: John Strong mistakes Matt Damon for Brad Pitt on Fox World Cup final broadcast
description: Fox play-by-play announcer John Strong had a celebrity mix-up during Sunday afternoon’s broadcast of the 2026 FIFA World Cup final between Spain and Argentina at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey. In the first half, the Fox broadcast showed actor Matt Damon in attendance. But Strong thought it was another high-profile actor, Brad Pitt.…
fullText:
Fox play-by-play announcer John Strong had a celebrity mix-up during Sunday afternoon’s broadcast of the 2026 FIFA World Cup final between Spain and Argentina at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey.

In the first half, the Fox broadcast showed actor Matt Damon in attendance. But Strong thought it was another high-profile actor, Brad Pitt.

“Brad Pitt also among the fans here,” Strong said on the Fox broadcast, alongside color commentator Stu Holden.

“Oh, it’s Matt Damon,” Strong realized. “That’s Matt Damon.”

Strong at least realized the gaffe quickly and apologized.

“Apologize to both,” Strong said.

The timing of this mix-up makes it even funnier, with Damon starring as Odysseus in Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey (which hit theaters on Friday), after Pitt starred in the blockbuster film Troy in 2004.

Damon was at the World Cup final with his wife, Luciana Barroso, who is from Argentina.

Odysseus pulled up to the World Cup final

Matt Damon’s wife, Luciana, is from Argentina

Unfortunately for them, the Lionel Messi-led Argentina squad wasn’t able to repeat as World Cup champions and lost 1-0 to Spain.

title: Spain win World Cup after Messi's Argentina fall flat
description: Spain won their second World Cup on Sunday, collecting the trophy from US President Donald Trump after Ferran Torres' extra-time strike broke the stubborn resistance of Lionel Messi's Argentina.Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni admitted Spain had deserved to win.
fullText:
Spain won their second World Cup on Sunday, collecting the trophy from US President Donald Trump after Ferran Torres' extra-time strike broke the stubborn resistance of Lionel Messi's Argentina.

More than 80,000 people crammed into the MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey, to witness a tense, tight final punctuated by cynical Argentine fouls that ended 1-0.

The defending champions struggled to compete with dominant Spain and were left with a mountain to climb when Enzo Fernandez was sent off in the 93rd minute for a second yellow card after a reckless challenge on defender Pau Cubarsi.

Argentina barely had an attempt on goal in the entire match but held on grimly, with goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez making a series of saves.

The 2010 winners, who had peppered their opponents' goal, finally broke the deadlock early in the second half of extra-time.

The goal came from a brilliant knock-back by Nico Williams for fellow substitute Torres, who smashed the ball high into the net in the 106th minute.

At the end, the Spain players cavorted on the pitch while Messi was in tears as he trudged up to collect his runners-up medal.

The ceremony was presided over by Trump, who was loudly booed as he walked onto the pitch at the end of the game with Gianni Infantino, the chief of world football's governing body FIFA.

Trump had to be asked by Infantino to move to the side as he lingered on stage while the Spanish team held the trophy aloft.

The 80-year-old even did a quick version of his trademark "Trump dance".

An emotional Spain coach Luis de la Fuente said his players were a "source of pride" for the nation.

Spain captain Rodri, who won the player of the tournament award, said it was hard to put his feelings into words, 14 months after he returned from a serious knee injury.

"I think now we're like in a cloud," he said. "It's been a very tough time. I just want the new generations to see my example as an opportunity -- when you go down you can rise again."

Messi, 39, may have played his last World Cup game and he can take enormous credit for dragging his ageing team to the final after they were pushed to the limit in knockout games against Egypt and England.

But on Sunday, he was largely shut out of the game as Spain imposed their smooth passing game and refused to shirk physical challenges.

The South Americans had been aiming to become the first team since Brazil in 1962 to win consecutive World Cups. But Spain were faster, slicker and younger.

Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni admitted Spain had deserved to win.

"They were the better side -- that's the truth -- but I'll cherish the memory of (this team), of what they achieved, and of the value of making it this far," said Scaloni, who later broke down in tears at his post-game press conference.

"We have to place immense value on this because it takes so much effort," he added.

Musical headliners Madonna, Shakira, Justin Bieber, BTS and Coldplay were joined by Brazilian soccer icons Ronaldinho and Ronaldo and "Ted Lasso" actors in the first-ever World Cup final half-time show.

The star-packed, Super Bowl-style extravaganza also featured a tribute to late football icon Pele, and a rendition of "Seven Nation Army" led by Gustavo Dudamel and featuring musicians from the US, Venezuela and Iran.

On Saturday, England beat France 6-4 in a high-scoring thriller to seal third place -- their best finish since they won the tournament in 1966.

Kylian Mbappe became the competition's all-time leading scorer with 22 goals.

The France forward also won the Golden Boot with 10 goals, two ahead of Messi, finishing as top scorer for the second World Cup in a row.

The build-up to the World Cup in Canada, Mexico and the United States was dominated by fears over sky-high prices, geopolitical tensions and concerns that brutal summer heat would make matches unplayable.

But the biggest, most complex edition of football's showpiece ever staged -- expanded from 32 teams to 48 -- will be remembered for thrilling football and a feel-good factor.

Trump has hailed the tournament as "the most successful sporting event, maybe in the history of the world".

title: Zlatan Ibrahimovic announces Fox decision after World Cup
description: Excitement arose among soccer fans around the world when it was announced that Zlatan Ibrahimovic will serve as an analyst for the FIFA World Cup. He worked alongside Alexi Lalas and Thierry Henry for Fox Sports’ coverage and gave entertaining…
fullText:
Excitement arose among soccer fans around the world when it was announced that Zlatan Ibrahimovic will serve as an analyst for the FIFA World Cup. He worked alongside Alexi Lalas and Thierry Henry for Fox Sports’ coverage and gave entertaining insights about games and players.

However, the legend announced after Sunday’s final that he will not return. He bid the network goodbye and stressed that he will not cover future World Cups.

MORE: Argentina World Cup run ends with wild brawl after whistle vs Spain

“”And this is also my farewell to the studio,” Ibrahimovic said. “This was the first and last time for me. So, take care, everybody.”

Ibrahimovic anchored the Los Angeles-based studio team, contributing to pregame, halftime and postgame shows. He gave highly entertaining analysis in true Zlatan fashion, endearing him more to fans who supported him throughout his playing career.

The 44-year-old hung his cleats up for good in 2022 following AC Milan’s final game of the season. He started his senior club career with Malmo FF before playing for Ajax, Juventus and Inter Milan. Ibrahimovic then moved to Barcelona, AC Milan, Paris Saint-German and Manchester United. He also suited up for the LA Galaxy before returning to Italy to end his career there.

Ibrahimovic won league titles in the Netherlands, Italy, Spain, France and England. He also suited up for Sweden in international competitions, establishing himself as one of the best forwards in the world. His flamboyant playing style and larger-than-life persona made him an influential figure in the sport.

title: World Cup 2026: Spain ends Argentina's reign, wins second title
description: Ferran Torres side-footed in an extra-time dagger to beat Argentina, leading the Spaniards to their second title Sunday in the 2026 World Cup Final.
fullText:
EAST RUTHERFORD, N.J., July 19 (UPI) -- Spanish defenders were a quiver of cobras, flooding Argentine attacks with venomous intent until Ferran Torres netted an extra-time strike to beat the defending champions 1-0 in Sunday's 2026 World Cup Final.

The Spaniards outshot the Argentines 20-2 in the instant classic at New York-New Jersey Stadium. More than 80,000 fans, including U.S. President Donald Trump and a cast of celebrities, witnessed the breathtaking soccer showcase in East Rutherford, N.J.

With the victory, Spain became the first country in history to win consecutive men's and women's World Cups. They also denied Argentina the distinction of becoming the third men's team to successfully defend its title.

"I'm sure that when we get to Spain we'll realize what a crazy thing we've done," said Spain goalie Unai Simon, who earned a record-extending seventh shutout, the most in World Cup history.

The Argentines, who played shorthanded in stoppage time and extra time after Enzo Fernandez drew his second yellow card, managed to fend off rampant Spanish attacks throughout the match until Torres finally broke through in the 106th minute.

The Spaniards held a 65% to 35% advantage in possession, using their 4-2-2-3 positioning to stretch Argentina's defense. They out-passed their foes 858-456.

Simon, who did not face a shot on target in the finale, received Golden Glove honors as the best goalie of the tournament.

Midfielder Rodri won the Golden Ball as the tournament's best player. Defender Pau Cubarsi was named Best Young Player.

Argentine goalie Emiliano Martinez recorded his first of 11 saves in the fifth minute. Simon picked the ball off of Lionel Messi's boot about a minute later.

La Roja continued to threaten, with additional attempts from Marc Cucurella and Lamine Yamal, among others. Their pinball-style passing put significant pressure on La Albiceleste, but Martinez produced diving saves behind a strong back line throughout regulation.

Fernandez, who drew his first yellow card in the 82nd minute, received his second in stoppage time, forcing Argentina to play with 10 men in extra time.

Spain appeared to finally break through in the 96th minute when Nico Williams buried a follow up shot, but that goal was disallowed due to a foul call.

Williams went on to play a vital role in the second period of extra time, serving up Torres' game winner. The Spanish forward ran toward the far post and used the back of his head to glance a deflection from the end line into the box.

The ball bounced softly in front of Torres, who used his left boot to drill a shot into the right side of the net.

Messi and the Argentines finally registered their first shot attempt in the 117th minute. They registered a second minutes later, but could not spoil the Spaniards' Sunday.

The Spaniards cashed in for $51 million, and overtook the Argentines as the No. 1 team in the FIFA world rankings for their efforts at the record-setting 48-team World Cup.

"I think, at the end, this goal was scored by 47 million people, not mine, nor from the rest of the team," Torres said. "Today, destiny was written. Destiny was made for us, for our win."

The Argentines received $34 million for their efforts. Messi, who did not meet with the media after the match, finished the tournament with eight goals, the second-most in the tournament behind Golden Boot winner Kylian Mbappe, who scored 10.

Mbappe, who also was named Golden Boot winner in 2022, became the first men's player to win the award twice.

title: From Spain with love: A World Cup final 50 million dreams in the making
description: Spain's victory in the World Cup sparked a celebration everyone should experience once in their life.
fullText:
EAST RUTHERFORD, N.J. — The aging toreador paced like he was being stalked by an unseen bull. He was wearing a pink rhinestone-spangled bullfighter's outfit and black hat — and authentic black-and-green Pumas, too —  and he simply couldn't stand still.

The last few minutes of the World Cup final — a final where Spain held a 1-0 lead that felt as fragile as glass — ticked away slowly, so slowly, and the pink toreador couldn't bear to watch. He wandered around the landing at the top of  MetLife Stadium's Section 202, ambled down the tunnel — at roughly the same pace, it must be said, as Leo Messi wanders the pitch — waiting for the last seconds of stoppage time to tick away. Along the tunnel above him hung a sign with gold letters reading, simply, THE FINAL.

And when those interminable five minutes finally came to an end, when the players on the field collapsed in joy and the fans in the stands whooped in delirium, the old toreador quietly made his way back to his seat, right next to his companion wearing a similar black-and-gold outfit, and watched as Spain celebrated, a look of serenity on his grizzled face.

They flowed into MetLife Stadium, tired (from standing in hours-long lines), poor (from paying four figures and up per ticket), huddled in masses of vivid red and sky blue, yearning to celebrate a World Cup victory but fearing that they were about to witness heartbreak on a worldwide scale.

There are moments you know as you're living them that you'll remember for the rest of your life, and the supporters of both Argentina and Spain who gathered in New Jersey spent the hours leading up to Sunday's World Cup final pinballing between joy and terror, confidence and dread.

"I'm a little nervous," said Javier Garrapiz, draped in a Spanish flag and with a quiver in his voice that suggested he might be more than just a little nervous. He and two friends stood near a merch stand, laughing to keep the fear at bay. "I was more relaxed this morning. Now I'm just nervous."

Nearby, an Argentine-born Bay Area resident by the name of Bernardo echoed the sentiment. "I have been confident," he said, a stack of emptied beer cups in his hand, "but now I am nervous." He was sporting Argentina's midnight-blue variant shirt — No. 10, of course — and the 56-year-old said he'd been a fan of Argentina's national team since he was a young boy.

Their tens of thousands of compatriots aligned with La Roja and La Albiceleste streamed into Jersey, following their beloved squads over the Garden State's highways and byways, journeying across vast expanses of asphalt parking lots — and, in at least one case, on foot across an interstate. Sure, among the 80,663 in attendance there were plenty of celebrities who'd helicoptered in for the chance to wave to the crowd on one of the big screens. But for the real fans — the ones who live and love and one day die with their national squads close to their hearts — this was more than just another trip to a match. This was a pilgrimage.

Argentina fans are crossing the highway to get to MetLife pic.twitter.com/J2ATFbqA2q

— Barstool Sports (@barstoolsports) July 19, 2026

There is a certain kind of fan, the foundation of a national team, who simply can't imagine not being in the arena for these moments. FIFA did all it could to test the depth of that love:  Astronomical ticket prices, capricious and debatable decisions, the nagging fear that what happens on the pitch is only half the battle. But through it all, through all the invasive sponsor activations, heart-stopping ticket prices, maddening bureaucratic meddling, and, finally, the massive lines to get into MetLife Stadium, the true fans came. And were rewarded.

FIFA manages the most popular and unifying event on the planet this side of religions. But even FIFA knows how to adopt a good idea or two for itself. Much like the NFL with the Super Bowl, FIFA introduced an overstuffed entertainment component to this year's final. Post Malone and Tom Cruise, among others, kicked off the festivities 80 minutes before the match began on Sunday, performing before a stadium that was well below capacity at the time.

That's likely because lines to get into the stadium were massive, thanks in part to increased security screening for President Donald Trump and also to some logistical misfires. Despite the fact that every other event at the stadium had gone off flawlessly, the final featured exponentially more headaches, and FIFA left people standing outside for hours waiting to get in.

If they arrived before kickoff, they saw some of the multiple gargantuan, field-sized flags that FIFA rolled out over the course of the day. (What happens to the flags? What can you do with a flag literally large enough to cover a house? What if you did cover your house with a flag, would that be an HOA violation? These are the things you begin to wonder as pre-game festivities drag on.)

Pregame entertainment also included Robbie Williams, dressing for the location by looking exactly like a member of Tony Soprano's crew. (One wonders how Tony et. al. would have dealt with FIFA muscling in on their territory. Again, the things you wonder while waiting for the damn match to begin.) After another round of self-congratulation from FIFA, including flags and massive F-I-F-A letters that required multiple people to tote, we finally got within sight of the main event. Bruce and Michael Buffer told us to get ready to rumble, but their act apparently hasn't translated to Spain or Argentina; the audience reaction was tepid at best.

And then, at long last, the match began.

In times of greatest anticipation and greatest stress, when you've spent days or weeks or years anticipating the moment, there comes a time when you're just flat-out tired. Exhausted from the potent cocktail of hope and dread and adrenalin that's coursed through your veins for what feels like forever, you almost can't believe the moment is here. See, for instance, our toreador, who seemed to be walking in a dream state.

He wasn't alone. Spain employed its death-by-a-thousand-passes attack to perfection, while Argentina wasn't able to rattle the cage of La Roja the way it had England in the semis. Neither technique exactly inspires passion in the surrounding supporters; all too often, fans are reduced to firing quick bursts of outrage at fouls, and little else.

The match thus rolled into halftime with a growing sense of dread looming over the stadium. The halftime show's canned messages of inoffensive optimism — "Never forget who you are!" "Live an inspired life" — did little to stem the fear that doom was approaching, and no, I'm not referring to Madonna's emergence from the southeast tunnel.

Spain, you see, had observed this script play out before. They'd watched from afar as Argentina hung around its 2026 World Cup matches, waiting for the right moment to strike. They'd seen how La Albiceleste had not led a single one of its knockout matches at the 90th minute, and yet managed to win every single one of those. They knew that as long as Messi loped, Argentina hoped.

A note on Messi, who spent most of the match wandering around the pitch like a sweat-pant-wearing retiree taking a slow after-dinner stroll. This is his game, has been for years now — Argentina plays 10 men on offense, 10 men on defense, as the joke goes — and it plays perfectly to Messi's greatest strength, the ability to suddenly rise to, and above, the moment.

Friday night, Messi participated in an event at Fanatics Fest where several other "GOATs" — Tom Brady, Novak Djokovic, and also the Kevins Hart and Durant — asked him softball questions. What was remarkable was that on a weekend where everyone else at Fanatics Fest tried their hardest to establish themselves as alphas, Messi — shorter than almost all of them, as physically imposing as an insurance adjuster — outshone them all, without raising his voice or even getting out of his chair.

His quiet confidence is his super power, his certainty that no matter the scenario, he can find a way through. And this match seemed like it was heading very much in that direction, with a late Messi goal — either in the last minutes of regulation or the precious few minutes of stoppage time — piercing the heart of the Spanish faithful.

But not this time. This time, Ferran Torres found the goal less than a minute into the second half of extra time, sending the La Roja fans gathered in the south end of the stadium into absolute ecstasy.

WOULD YOU BELIEVE IT?! SPAIN TAKES THE LEAD IN EXTRA TIME 🇪🇸🤯

Ferran Torres gives La Roja the lead!!! pic.twitter.com/hLso0tg3LI

— FOX Sports (@FOXSports) July 19, 2026

A note on the fan bases: Argentina probably held a 4-1 edge in terms of raw numbers, with at least 90 percent of those sporting Messi jerseys. But Spain matched the nervous, distressed Argentine fans in sheer volume, and when Torres drilled his shot — and Argentina missed two last-minute heart-stoppers — well, that was enough to start a celebration that will last for years to come.

Friends, I hope that one day, you enjoy a day as ecstatic and blissful as those Spain fans did on Sunday afternoon just after 6:00 p.m. Eastern. The roja-clad fans exploded in laughter and happy tears, cheering and chanting, bouncing up and down and grabbing one another in delirium. The Spanish embraced everyone in sight, from photographers to security guards trying valiantly to keep the aisles clear. I got hugged more times on Sunday than I did at my own wedding. (Got more beer thrown on me, too.)

All around me, fans filmed tearful selfies with confetti explosions and fireworks in the background. Others FaceTimed distant friends and relatives, bringing them into this glorious moment. They chanted in triplets – "Es! Pa! Ña!" — and they (mostly) gave their Argentine colleagues conciliatory claps on the shoulder.

An Argentina fan named JC looked out at the scene with resignation on his face. "The refs were not so good," he said, but his heart didn't seem to be into it. He took a few more pictures, and then walked down the tunnel to leave Spain to its celebrations.

Not everything proceeded so smoothly. The end of the match brought a wave of assaults and other security incidents, one  law enforcement official told me. But it was nothing that security couldn't handle: "Compared to Jets fans," he said, smiling, "this is nothing."

As the celebration wound down, the stadium speakers played "Un Beso y una Flor" ("A Kiss and A Flower"), and the gathered fans swayed and sang in tearful unison. It's a perfect song for the moment, a tale of departures and memories, of holding onto the best moments of the past as you push forward into the future.

Beyond the sea, there will be a place
Where the sun shines brighter every morning
As I leave, a kiss and a flower 
An 'I love you,' a caress, and a goodbye

The toreadors, who spoke no English, sang along, their thoughts their own but their expressions content.

"Incredible," a Spanish fan named Santi said as he looked out on the field. He hadn't had a ticket or a flight here as recently as Friday, but he found a way. "I'm so full of happiness," he said with a smile. Asked what his plans were next, his smile got even wider. "I'm going to Times Square!" And then his buddy dumped a beer on his head. All was perfect in Santi's world.

MetLife Stadium got the last word, signing off the night with the traditional playing of Sinatra's "New York, New York," and even if the Spanish crowd didn't know all the lyrics, they got the spirit of the moment. They took their last photos of the field and the massive World Cup trophy, holding on to the scene as the sun set beyond the stadium's walls.

They made it here, and now, there's no doubt — they can make it anywhere.

title: Ecstatic celebrations as Spaniards fete World Cup triumph
description: Thousands of ecstatic Spanish fans in Madrid bellowed out the Queen song "We are the Champions" following Spain's victory in the World Cup final on Sunday.One who did is 40-year-old John Molina, who remembers the first World Cup victory, in the final against the Netherlands 16 years ago, which he said was "a dream" at that time.
fullText:
Thousands of ecstatic Spanish fans in Madrid bellowed out the Queen song "We are the Champions" following Spain's victory in the World Cup final on Sunday.

Even passengers on an Iberia flight were treated to the anthem by the British rock band.

The Iberia pilot could barely contain himself after safely landing the plane at Madrid airport, announcing: "We are world champions, ladies and gentlemen, dear passengers."

La Roja -- as the national side are known -- beat 2022 champions Argentina 1-0 after extra time in New Jersey to secure their second 'star' having also won in 2010.

No-one in Madrid was likely to get much sleep such were the celebrations in the city which erupted close to midnight (2200 GMT) when the final whistle was blown on a match that the Spanish dominated before Ferran Torres secured victory with a goal in extra time.

The horns of cars blared, though they were not going far with the hordes of people who flooded the streets cheering raucously and blowing vuvuzelas -- the instrument that became a feature of the 2010 World Cup hosted in South Africa.

Thousands dressed in the team strip, brandishing the national flag, and with their faces painted had already gathered to watch the final on a giant screen in the centre of the capital city.

Flares and fire works also lit up the sky and 25-year-old Diego gasped with disbelief that for the second time in his relatively short life his country had won the biggest trophy in the sport.

"We won the Euro in 2008, then the World Cup: two years ago we won the Euro again and now once more we have won the World Cup," he purred.

He said he would grab some sleep before awaiting the arrival of the returning heroes because "we must also celebrate that".

Around one million people are expected to line the parade route, with additional security and transport measures planned to handle the crowds.

"It is crazy to think we are singing it because at one point we no longer believed it would happen," said 26-year-old Alejandra Silva, who had to raise her voice to be heard.

Her friend Angela Lambea, 30, said it would have been unfair if superstar Lionel Messi and his side -- whose overly physical tactics eventually resulted in Enzo Fernandez being sent off -- had gone on to win.

"We deserved it, we played really well, we had the ball the whole time, Argentina barely touched it. We are totally overcome with emotion," she said.

Both of them were keen to carry on the celebrations, though, with the caveat that they had to work on Monday.

Cries of "Campeones, campeones!" ("Champions, champions!") and "Que viva España!" rang out.

Indeed their celebrations were mirrored across a football-mad country of around 50 million people although some of the younger revellers would have had no memory of the earlier glory nights.

One who did is 40-year-old John Molina, who remembers the first World Cup victory, in the final against the Netherlands 16 years ago, which he said was "a dream" at that time.

He already has his eyes on where he will carry on celebrating: "We are all going to Cibeles", the square where Real Madrid supporters gather to fete their club's trophies.

Molina and others may as well stay on -- though the detritus of Sunday night and Monday morning's celebrations will have had to be cleared away quickly -- as Rodri, Lamine Yamal and their teammates will end their victory parade there later on Monday.

It promises to be another party to remember.

title: Lamine Yamal completes football at 19 as Argentina betray Messi
description: Spain's Lamine Yamal takes the glory of World Cup victory as the great Lionel Messi's evening ends in tears.
fullText:
When brilliant Spain teenager Lamine Yamal embraced a tearful Lionel Messi at the end of the World Cup final, it looked and felt symbolic. A passing of the torch.

Messi, at 39, gazed long and hard at Argentina's fans packed around the New York New Jersey Stadium as they sang his name after what may be the last time he graces the game's biggest stage.

And, with the world watching, it was Lamine Yamal who offered the great Argentina icon consolation before they fell into each other's arms.

Barcelona's number 10 embracing their most legendary number 10.

A different image to the amazing photo of a 20-old Messi bathing a six-month-old Lamine Yamal in September 2007 as part of a charity calendar raffle that went viral once 

The baby boy is now a man, and Messi may be at the end of his World Cup journey.

Lamine Yamal - two decades younger and still in the infancy of his career - has effectively completed international football by lifting the World Cup two years after he came to wider prominence by winning the Euros when Spain beat England.

He, like Messi a product of Barcelona, came into the tournament troubled by a hamstring injury, but ended it as the youngest player - at 19 years and six days - to win both the World Cup and Euros.

And one of the key storylines contained within this disappointing World Cup final was how Lamine Yamal's influence was assisted by the purist strategies of his Spain team-mates - while Messi was betrayed by Argentina's provocative approach.

It yielded nothing, which was what it deserved.

Spain and Lamine Yamal have grown through the tournament to leave him with the world at his feet and football's two greatest prizes in his pocket.

As well as having glorious natural gifts, he is also Spain's lucky charm. They completed their domination of the international stage with Ferran Torres' decisive 106th-minute strike in extra time.

Lamine Yamal has yet to lose for his country in the 28 competitive matches he has played. He has started 13 games at major tournaments and won all of them - the longest 100% win rate of any European player across major tournaments when starting.

He also became - along with 19-year-old Spain team-mate Pau Cubarsi - just the fourth and fifth teenagers to start a World Cup final and win, after Pele for Brazil in 1958, Italy defender Giuseppe Bergomi in 1982 and Kylian Mbappe with France in 2018.

Spain coach Luis de la Fuente spoke afterwards of his "sincere gratitude" to Lamine Yamal.

"He has played great football that will help him to mature and be even a greater footballer than he already is," he said.

"He really sacrificed for the collective good. This is very important in my squad. In my opinion he played a great World Cup."

And this is not simply a special talent, it is a special talent with a special temperament.

He shrugged off Argentina's obvious attempts to intimidate him as he regularly came into close contact with Nicolas Tagliafico in particular.

Former England goalkeeper and BBC Sport pundit Joe Hart summed it up well.

"Not only did Lamine Yamal not react," he said. "He stood up to it. He was not rattled and he did not back down. He had the entire team trying to smash him.

"He just stayed real cool. It was real standout performance from him.

"Lamine Yamal is 19 years old. What a beautiful day for him. He's 19 and has completed football."

With virtually his whole career in front of him, and taking in the experience and influence of Spain team-mates such as his captain and player of the tournament Rodri, there is every chance Lamine Yamal will add more successes like this before he is done.

Sixteen titles in five years - why Spain are a dominant force

Spain's 'victory for football' over 'embarrassing' Argentina

Trump, Bieber and Shakira - a World Cup final like never before

The joy of Lamine Yamal was the sharpest of contrasts to Messi, who looked heartbroken as he stared into those massed ranks of Argentina supporters, the realisation perhaps dawning that this would be the final time he would take such adoration at the World Cup.

Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni, like Messi trying to win a second successive World Cup, became highly emotional when asked whether this was the legendary attacker's final game in the tournament - while also reflecting on his own future with his contract due to expire in December.

Scaloni said he had not spoken to Messi, before bursting into tears himself then leaving the news conference.

Messi's cause was not helped here by Argentina's confrontational approach from virtually the first whistle, which left their inspiration and leader effectively a passenger for most of the game.

Spain's victory was a victory for football because Argentina appeared to be focused on anything but that from early on, when Slovenian referee Slavko Vincic exercised the sort of leniency that has infuriated opponents throughout the World Cup.

Alexis Mac Allister's poor challenge on Dani Olmo received nothing more than a brief admonishment, prompting Spain fans to ironically chant the name of Fifa president Gianni Infantino. It was another nod to the conspiracy theorists who claim Argentina's road to the final has been smoothed out by officialdom.

It was 40 minutes before Argentina defender Lisandro Martinez received the game's first yellow card for a foul on Mikel Oyarzabal - greeted with heavy sarcasm by many inside this vast bowl of a stadium.

To see Argentina's obvious talent, which they displayed by overpowering England in the semi-final, reduced to such spoiling tactics was a dismal sight, hardly designed to aid Messi's genius and influence.

Messi even got in the act by suggesting the referee should send off Spain's Marc Cucurella for talking with his hand over his mouth - punishable by a red card if employed in a confrontational manner at this World Cup.

It came after the low point of Argentina's performance, Enzo Fernandez's senseless charge at Cubarsi just before the game went into extra time. The Chelsea midfielder was not even looking at the ball when he flattened his opponent. A second yellow card was fully deserved.

Argentina were the first team in the tournament's history not to have a shot in 90 minutes of a World Cup final. When it did come, in the 117th minute and with desperation mounting, Messi fired off target.

Spain had 12 shots on target, Argentina had none in the entire game.

In a shocking first half, Argentina committed more fouls than passes in the final third of the pitch. Nine as opposed to eight. It summed their approach.

Argentina's players joined Messi in shedding tears after the final whistle, though even that ended in an undignified scrum between players and officials from either side.

Leandro Paredes - as he was too often after coming on as substitute for the second half - was the aggressor, grabbing Eric Garcia by the throat. He was also involved as Spain's Gavi was thrown to the ground.

This outstanding World Cup should not be remember for such an unsavoury finale.

Spain may not have been at their best in the final, but they have developed throughout the World Cup, from the shock of being held to a draw in their opening game by Cape Verde to ultimate glory.

The World Cup got the winners it deserved, and in Lamine Yamal it has a star of future tournaments.

Messi's unforgettable World Cup era may be over - but the Lamine Yamal years could go on for a long time to come.

title: Sixteen titles in five years - why Spain are a dominant force
description: We look at what's behind Spain's dominance after they become the first nation to be men's and women's World Cup champions at the same time.
fullText:
After being crowned European champions two years ago in Berlin, La Roja are now world champions having beaten Argentina 1-0 in the World Cup final in New Jersey on Sunday.

They achieved the same feat back in 2010, two years after winning Euro 2008.

Spain's latest success continues their remarkable dominance in the men's and women's game.

They are the first nation to be men's and women's World Cup winners at the same time – La Roja overcoming England in the Women's World Cup final in Sydney in 2023.

That's not all. The men won the Nations League in 2023 and gold at the 2024 Olympics, while the women won the Nations League in both 2024 and 2025.

In fact, in the last five years, Spain have now won an astonishing 16 world and European titles from under-17 to senior level.

"It is a dominance," Spanish football expert Guillem Balague told BBC Sport.

"It's like the perfect storm. Culturally, we do things a certain way. Everybody believes in it. The important thing is that we don't stop.

"It's another Everest because the men won the World Cup in 2010 two years after winning the Euros. Now we've done it again."

Women's football (11 titles 2022-2026)

Senior: 1 x World Cup (2023); 2 x Nations Leagues (2024, 2025).

Under-20: 1 x World Cup (2022).

Under-19: 5 x Under-19 European champions (2022, 2023, 2024, 2025, 2026).

Under-17: 1 x World Cup (2022); 1 x European champions (2024).

Men's football (5 titles 2022-2026)

Senior: 1 x World Cup (2026); 1 x European champions (2024); 1 x Nations League (2023).

Under-23: 1 x Olympic gold medal winners (2024).

Under-19: 1 x European champions (2026).

When Spain appointed Luis de la Fuente as head coach in December 2022, few expected him to deliver the Nations League (2023), European Championship (2024) and World Cup (2026) in so short a time frame.

Spain had just been knocked out of the World Cup by Morocco in the last 16 in Qatar and the federation needed a replacement after Luis Enrique left his role.

"They went with this guy [De La Fuente] who understood the style and the system," added Balague.

"It was a very risky decision - I don't think anyone thought he would be so successful."

It appeared risky because De La Fuente took charge of Spain, who have won three of the past five men's European Championships - (2008, 2012, 2024) - without having managed a big club.

After retiring as a player in 1994, De La Fuente spent 15 years in different roles with a succession of different clubs, including managing in the lower Spanish leagues, youth roles and assistant coach positions - before he took charge of Spain's youth teams.

"De la Fuente knew most of these players from the academies and they are growing as a team," former Spain midfielder Juan Mata told BBC Sport.

"This is not only a team for the present, but also a team for the future."

Since being appointed senior boss after stints in charge of the under-19s, under-21s and at Olympic level, De La Fuente has implemented a culture of respect for their rivals, for the process and preaching patience and calmness.

"Most of you are younger than me and you need to respect experience," the 65-year-old told journalists at his pre-match news conference in New York on Friday.

"Once we finished the game, there were footballers who said 'coach, now what else is there to win as we have won everything in all the categories'.

"We came to the conclusion that what remains is the next match in September because this team are relentless."

The success of the national teams and Spanish coaches has spilled over to club football.

Despite Pep Guardiola's decision to leave Manchester City after 10 trophy-packed years, at least a quarter of the managers who start the 2026-27 Premier League season are Spanish.

Spain are now unbeaten in their last 38 matches in all competitions, the longest ever run by any European or South American team in history.

Spain's 'victory for football' over 'embarrassing' Argentina

Watch as Spain lift World Cup trophy after beating Argentina

Meanwhile, the women's game in Spain has thrived in the last decade and is also starting to reel in big successes in the high-pressure games.

The country's victory over England three years ago in the Women's World Cup final came in just their third appearance at the tournament, having made their debut in 2015.

Spain's women then followed that up by winning back-to-back Nations Leagues in 2024 and 2025.

"The changes in women's football in Spain have been especially significant," former player Maria Garrido, who is now a journalist, told BBC Sport in 2024.

"Ten years ago, when I played for FC Barcelona, there was no La Masia [the famed academy at Barcelona] for girls. We covered our own transport costs, our parents brought us to training, and we didn't earn any money.

"But in the last five years, the situation has improved dramatically. There has been a substantial push to promote women's football, including the creation of more youth categories, better facilities and conditions, and the establishment of a dedicated football academy for girls.

"This transformation has not only revolutionised the sport but also elevated the respect for women's football in Spain."

These successes for both the men's and women's teams followed lifting numerous titles at youth level over the last decade.

The future looks bright, with many believing Spain's dominance will continue.

In Lamine Yamal and Pau Cubarsi, the men have two outstanding young talents who have picked up their first World Cup winners' medal at the age of 19.

Vicky Lopez, who plays for the national women's team, is a three-time Champions League winner with Barcelona at the same age.

Forward Salma Paralluelo has also represented her country more than 50 times before the age of 23.

Yamal became the third-youngest player to play in a World Cup final, after Brazil's Pele in 1958 (17 years, 249 days) and Italy's Giuseppe Bergomi in 1982 (18 years and 201 days).

With Cubarsi also starting on Sunday at the age of 19 years and 178 days, Spain were the first side to feature two teenagers in a single World Cup final.

"Spain have got a way of playing that no one in the world has proven they can get anywhere near," former England goalkeeper Joe Hart told BBC Sport after the win over Argentina.

"No one laid a glove on them at this World Cup, [they faced] just 10 shots on target against them in the whole tournament."

Meanwhile, France 1998 World Cup winner Thierry Henry told Fox Sports that Spain have the potential to dominate for years to come.

"I want to give credit to the system and what they put in place," he said.

"Because Spain never used to win like that. And now they win at every level."

title: Swansea close in on deal for Motherwell's Just
description: Swansea City hope to tie up a deal for Motherwell's Elijah Just after he impressed for New Zealand at the World Cup.
fullText:
Swansea City are close to agreeing a deal to sign Motherwell's New Zealand international winger Elijah Just.

The 26-year-old played in all three of the All Whites' games at this summer's World Cup and scored three goals.

Championship side Swansea will have to pay a sizeable fee - and break Motherwell's club record for a sum collected - to secure the services of Just, who has also been linked with Celtic.

Just joined Motherwell in 2025 having spent the majority of his career playing in Denmark.

He made 43 appearances for the Fir Park club, scored seven goals and was nominated for Premiership player of the year.

Just would join fellow New Zealand international Marko Stamenic at Swansea should he complete a move to Wales.

He would also be reunited with summer signing Stephen Welsh, who spent last season on loan at Motherwell from Celtic.

title: Argentina boss Scaloni leaves news conference in tears talking about his future
description: Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni leaves his post-match news conference after Sunday's World Cup final defeat by Spain in tears.
fullText:
Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni left his post-match news conference in tears when talking about his future after Sunday's World Cup final defeat by Spain.

Scaloni, 48, was bidding to become only the second manager in history to retain the trophy after overseeing Argentina's 2022 triumph in Qatar.

But European champions Spain proved too strong, as Ferran Torres' extra-time strike saw Luis de la Fuente's side add to their European crown.

Scaloni's contract expires in December and he was overcome with emotion when asked about his future.

"I will talk to the president," he said. "I have an idea of what I would like to do. I will see out my contract and I feel the need, I don't know if something as big can be done. We need to speak this through.

"I am thankful to the president for bringing me this chance of being in this place where I am at the moment. It was a dream place for everyone. We have tried up until the very last minute to give our utmost, the staff and players. I believe it's only fair that I can take this time for myself to think this through."

Scaloni talked through his tears, adding "bear with me, I don't know if I can continue".

"This place is wonderful, it is a dream place," he said. "We would never have imagined, including my staff, that we would be in this place.

"In order to continue you need a great deal of things, especially resetting your mind, rebooting, creating a group like this again. That is very difficult to create again."

He eventually stood up and apologised before leaving the room.

"It's really painful, I am very sorry."

title: Shakira, Madonna, Justin Bieber and BTS perform at colourful World Cup half-time show
description: Robbie Williams and Jennifer Hudson also performed, with Kylie Jenner and Timothee Chalamet among the celebrities in the crowds.
fullText:
Pop giants Madonna, BTS, Shakira and Justin Bieber have performed for thousands of spectators at the World Cup final in a half-time show to rival the Superbowl.

Madonna, who sung her hit song Music - seemed to appear from tunnels under the arena, joined by Brazilian football legends Ronaldo and Ronaldinho.

The performances came in a half-time that ran to 27 minutes and 22 seconds - the longest in World Cup history.

Watch as Spain beat Argentina to win World Cup for second time in history

The length of the half-time show had already caused controversy, with the International Football Association Board (Ifab) rules stating players are only entitled to a maximum 15-minute stoppage.

Colombian pop star Shakira and Nigerian musician Burna Boy reunited on stage after performing together at the tournament's opening ceremony in Mexico City last month.

K-Pop supergroup BTS, meanwhile, have been making their comeback from a three-year music hiatus to complete their mandatory military service in South Korea.

Several celebrities were spotted watching on, including Kylie Jenner and her boyfriend Timothee Chalamet, as well as Beyonce and Jay-Z.

Earlier, a range of performances began the sporting event's closing ceremony, including Jennifer Hudson singing the national anthem and a speech from actor Tom Cruise.

Streamer IShowSpeed took to the field at the New York New Jersey Stadium first, alongside drummers and dancers in vibrant tracksuits.

Nicole Scherzinger, Robbie Williams and Italian singer Laura Pausini also performed together, while rapper and singer Post Malone also performed his hit song Wow.

Ahead of kick-off, multi-award-winning singer and actor Jennifer Hudson performed the US national anthem, accompanied by a live band.

After the performances, Tom Cruise took to the stage to introduce the match.

"From every corner of the globe, we have witnessed greatness, and we have shared in moments of joy," he told the crowd. "Football is a language spoken without words, a force that unites people."

Italian singer Pausini told the BBC she had goosebumps when Williams invited her to sing alongside him.

But not everyone was thrilled by the performances half-time performance.

Former England player Wayne Rooney, who was part of the commentary team for the BBC, gave his instant reaction: "I like a lot of them artists, but I thought it was crap."

title: Mbappe first to win Golden Boot twice as Rodri named World Cup's best player
description: Kylian Mbappe has become the first player to win the World Cup Golden Boot twice after earning the award for top scorer at the 2026 tournament, while Spain's Rodri lands the Golden Ball as the best player.
fullText:
Kylian Mbappe has become the first player to win the World Cup Golden Boot twice after earning the award for top scorer at the 2026 tournament.

The France forward netted 10 goals in eight games to edge out Lionel Messi in a high-quality race.

Having struck eight times to finish top scorer at Qatar 2022, Mbappe has won the Golden Boot in back-to-back World Cups.

The 27-year-old was the first man to reach double figures for World Cup goals since Gerd Muller for West Germany in 1970.

Only Just Fontaine, who hit 13 for France in 1958, and Hungary's Sandor Kocsis with 11 in 1954, have scored more individual goals in a single tournament.

Mbappe is also now the men's World Cup all-time top scorer with 22 goals - one ahead of Messi.

World champions Spain swept the rest of the awards, including captain Rodri winning the Golden Ball as the tournament's best player.

Mbappe moved clear of Messi on Saturday with two goals in the bronze match, although he could not stop France falling to a 6-4 defeat against England and finishing fourth.

"When you score that many goals in the World Cup, it certainly takes you to a whole new level," he said after Saturday's match in Miami.

"But I would have preferred not to be the top scorer and to have played in the final. It's good for my legacy once I've retired, but right now, that's not what's on my mind."

Mbappe also scored twice against Senegal and Iraq in the group stage, as well as Sweden in the last 32.

He followed that with strikes against Paraguay in the last 16 - the 70th-minute winner from the penalty spot - and against Morocco in the quarter-finals.

Messi could have overtaken the Real Madrid forward with a hat-trick in the World Cup final on Sunday.

However, the 39-year-old could not find the net in New Jersey as his Argentina side were beaten 1-0 by Spain, and ended the competition with eight goals in as many matches.

England's Jude Bellingham and Erling Haaland of Norway scored seven times, while France forward Ousmane Dembele and English captain Harry Kane followed them in the list with six.

Manchester City midfielder Rodri, a key figure in Spain's run to a second men's World Cup triumph, was named best player.

The 30-year-old received the Golden Ball from Fifa president Gianni Infantino on the field at the MetLife Stadium following the final in New Jersey.

It was the third major individual accolade of his career, having won the Ballon d'Or in 2023 and then Uefa Euro 2024 Player of the Tournament after Spain's final triumph against England two years ago.

Messi won the Silver Ball as the second-best player, with Mbappe named Bronze Ball winner.

Sixteen titles in five years - why Spain are a dominant force

Lamine Yamal completes football at 19 as Argentina betray Messi

Trump, Bieber and Shakira - a World Cup final like never before

Spain's Unai Simon claimed the Golden Glove award for best goalkeeper after conceding just once in eight games.

The Athletic Club stopper kept seven clean sheets and was only beaten by Belgium's Charles de Ketelaere in the quarter-final tie.

The 29-year-old was picked ahead of Arsenal's Premier League title winner David Raya as Spain's number one, and repaid manager Luis de la Fuente's faith with a series of solid performances as they were crowned champions.

Pau Cubarsi, the 19-year-old Barcelona centre-back, received the award for the tournament's best young player.

He was a key figure in the Spanish defence that only conceded once all tournament and restricted Argentina to just two shots in the final with none on target.

Cubarsi played every single minute for Spain as they emulated their 2010 World Cup success in South Africa.

title: Who made the difference for Spain? World Cup final player ratings
description: BBC Sport journalist Neil Johnston rates the players after the World Cup final between Spain and Argentina.
fullText:
Spain have won the World Cup after beating Argentina 1-0 in the final.

BBC Sport journalist Neil Johnston assesses how the players performed in New Jersey - and you can see how BBC Sport readers rated them at the bottom of the page too.

Unai Simon: Came charging outside his box in the opening minutes which almost led to disaster before recovering to help Spain add the World Cup to their European triumph of two-years ago. 7

Marc Cucurella: Drove forward on numerous occasions and went close with a rare first-half attempt which whistled just wide. 7

Aymeric Laporte: Has been a rock at the back throughout the tournament – and so he was once again as Spain recorded a seventh clean sheet in eight games. 7

Pau Cubarsi: Once again provided a calm presence in defence as he has done all tournament. Only the second player on record (from 1966) to complete 100-plus passes in a World Cup final. 8

Pedro Porro: Followed up on his solid display in the semi-final to play a part in the move that produced the winning goal. 7

Fabian Ruiz: Worked hard in the engine room as Argentina struggled to impose their authority. 7

Rodri: Another controlled midfield performance from the captain as he helped his country to their first World Cup triumph since 2010. 7

Alex Baena: Put himself about and made a nuisance of himself. 7

Dani Olmo: Created chances and made a nuisance of himself on another glorious day for Spanish football after the Euros success of 2024 and the Nations League triumph of 2023. 7

Lamine Yamal: Ends the tournament with a World Cup winners medal at the age of 19. Will there be more to come? Had the first chance of the final after finding space inside the box. 7

Mikel Oyarzabal: Has been influential upfront for Spain but was kept quiet for large parts. Ends a glorious tournament as Spain's leading scorer with five goals. 7

Pedri (62 mins): Came on to bring energy into the side. 7

Ferran Torres (62 mins): Injected pace into Spain's play and was first to react when the ball fell to him in extra-time to seal the biggest prize of all for his country. 8

Nico Williams (75 mins): What an impact. Williams has had to remain patient at this tournament and has been limited to a string of substitute appearances. Spain looked dangerous each time he had the ball at his feet and played a huge role in the goal. 9

Mikel Merino (75 mins): Has proved to be an impact substitute at this tournament and again played his part on a glorious day for Spain. 6

Eric Garcia (99 mins): Worked hard and put himself about after coming on in extra-time. 6

Martin Zubimendi (99 mins): Did what he needed to as Spain wrote another glorious chapter in their football history. 6

Listen: Football Daily - Spain win the World Cup

Emiliano Martinez: Produced a fine save to keep out Lamine Yamal's free-kick right at the end of normal time. 6

Nicolas Tagliafico: Helped frustrate Spain for large spells before Argentina's 10-men were undone. 6

Lisandro Martinez: Booked for a foul from behind on Mikel Oyarzabal. Then replaced by Nicolas Otamendi before half-time after picking up an injury. 5

Cristian Romero: Helped ensure Mikel Oyarzabal – Spain's top scorer at this World Cup – was quiet. Booked for dissent. 6

Gonzalo Montiel: Showed great reactions to save a goal after Dani Olmo's low cross. 6

Nico Gonzalez: Replaced at half-time after Argentina failed to register an attempt in the first half. 5

Alexis Mac Allister: Fortunate to escape a caution after a late challenge on Dani Olmo. 5

Enzo Fernandez: Shocking challenge on Pau Cubarsi which resulted in a second yellow card and dismissal – Argentina's first red card at a World Cup since 2006. 4

Rodrigo De Paul: Struggled to influence the game and was replaced midway through the second half. 5

Julian Alvarez: Did not impose his authority on a day that belonged to Spain. 5

Lionel Messi: If this was his final appearance on the World Cup stage, what a sad end for the legend. Struggled to seriously test the Spain defence and finished the tournament on eight goals – two behind France's Kylian Mbapppe. 6

Nicolas Otamendi (41 mins): Came on towards the end of the first half for Lisandro Martinez and tried to make things happen before Argentina were undone by Enzo Fernandez's red card. 5

Leandro Paredes (46 mins): He had only been on the pitch a few minutes when he was booked for shoving Rodri – and then was involved in ugly scenes at full time. 3

Nahuel Molina (50 mins): Disappointing end to his World Cup as Argentina came unstuck in the final. 5

Facundo Medina (70 mins): Unable to help his country as the best team won. 4

Giovanni Simeone (70 mins): Unable to make an impact as the Copa America winners struggled and Simeone could not get into get into the game. 4

Marcos Senesi (102 mins): Could not impact the game as Argentina conceded for the sixth straight game and lost the final. 4

title: Trump, Bieber and Shakira - a World Cup final like never before
description: The 2026 World Cup final may not linger long in the memory for what happened on the pitch but it will for all that surrounded it, with an appearance by President Trump, the longest half-time in football and songs by a host of stars.
fullText:
The 2026 World Cup final might not be remembered as a superb football match but it will at least go down in history for all that surrounded it.

There was the longest half-time in history, featuring performances from artists including Madonna and Justin Bieber, a pre-kick-off speech from Hollywood A-lister Tom Cruise, an appearance by US president Donald Trump and fireworks. Lots of fireworks - all in broad daylight.

Spain won the trophy for the second time, beating Argentina through a Ferran Torres goal in extra time but there was little else to talk about in the game itself. Plenty happened around the game though, with these some of the most memorable moments.

Spain's 'victory for football' over 'embarrassing' Argentina

Lamine Yamal completes football at 19 as Argentina betray Messi

Shakira, Madonna, Justin Bieber and BTS perform at colourful World Cup half-time show

This World Cup was co-hosted by the USA, Mexico and Canada but it has been the United States which has garnered much of the attention, particularly around comments or actions by Trump.

He previously stated that this has been a World Cup like no other - and he's certainly not wrong about that, not least with the controversy of US striker Folarin Balogun avoiding a ban despite being sent off .

That came after Trump admitted he had spoken to Fifa president Gianni Infantino, asking him to review the sending off. The lifting of the suspension meant Balogun was able to play for the USA in the last-16 loss to Belgium.

Perhaps just as surprising was Trump's lack of attendance at games throughout the tournament. He had not been to a single match prior to the final, meaning all eyes were on his appearance at New York New Jersey Stadium on Sunday.

His arrival was visible to those in the stadium as he was in one of three helicopters that flew overhead before doing a circuit of it and then landing.

He appeared to take his seat - alongside Infantino - about 20 minutes before kick-off, just in time to watch Robbie Williams and Nicole Scherzinger perform in the closing ceremony.

He was later seen in the first half being shown the trophy.

As well as Trump's appearance, the other big talking point in the lead up to the final was the Super Bowl-style half-time show.

While the mid-game break is usually for players to recover from first-half exertion, coaches to give a team-talk and fans to buy food and drinks, this time it was extended to allow a mini-concert to take place.

There were some big names featured - Madonna, BTS, Coldplay and Shakira, whose song 'Dai Dai' is the official anthem of the World Cup - all starred, while there was also an appearance by Justin Bieber.

After getting a pep talk from Ted Lasso and his assistant coach Beard, Bieber changed the pace of the otherwise energetic performances with an acoustic guitar version of 'Everything Hallelujah'.

The half-time interval lasted 27 minutes, but did it work? The reaction among fans was a bit mixed - but inside the Match of the Day studio, the assessment by former England striker Wayne Rooney was pretty blunt.

"I thought it was crap," he said. "I thought the performances were bad. The timings were bad. Just let us play football."

Meanwhile, the closing ceremony before the match was similar in style to many other such ceremonies with loud music, dancing on the pitch though with the addition too of a somewhat dramatic speech by actor Cruise.

For neutrals, one of the most-anticipated post-match moments centred around how visible a part Trump would play in the trophy presentation.

At the end of last year's Club World Cup final, Trump got caught up with the Chelsea celebrations after presenting the trophy to captain Reece James.

On that occasion, having handed the main prize over, he remained on the stage for a few seconds alongside James and goalkeeper Robert Sanchez before eventually departing to the rear.

"They told me that he was going to present the trophy and then exit the stage... but he wanted to stay," said James, who was part of England's squad for this year's World Cup.

At the end of Sunday's World Cup final, Trump - who had joined Infantino in handing out the medals to both the winning and losing players - did linger again for the trophy presentation, although not for quite as long as he did for the Club World Cup.

There had also been boos when Trump and Infantino emerged for the trophy ceremony while at least one player also appeared to choose not to shake Trump's hand, with Argentina defender Cristian Romero walking straight past him after receiving his medal from Infantino.

This was certainly a glamorous World Cup final and there was no shortage of fireworks - they went off before kick-off, during the half-time show and after Spain's trophy lift.

But the hours beforehand will have felt anything but glamorous as heightened security meant those getting into the ground faced long queues.

With Trump attending in person, the United States secret service took over security for the game.

Gates for spectators opened at 11:00 local time - four hours before kick-off but stadium staff, performers and media had been arriving since much earlier.

Confusion over access gates had led to some members of the media being told to go to one entrance, only to be told they had to go back and start again at the queue they had been in previously.

Bag checks were also more thorough than in previous games, contributing to the length of time people waited to get in.

Despite that, congestion was eventually eased and the game kicked off without any delays.

title: Victory for football? Spain beat Argentina to win World Cup
description: It might seem like a trite cliche, but you won't find many neutrals disagreeing with that verdict on the World Cup final.
fullText:
Describing a result as a 'victory for football' might seem like a trite cliche - but you won't find many neutrals disagreeing with that description of Spain's win in the 2026 World Cup final.

When Ferran Torres slammed in the extra-time winner, and Rodri lifted the trophy high in New Jersey, it is hard to imagine many will have had sympathy for Argentina.

Spain triumphed in a battle of endurance, for players and spectators, to beat the Argentines and win their second men's World Cup.

On every football metric - and, some would say, morality too - this was the right result.

"There is no doubt that the right team won," former England captain Alan Shearer said on BBC One. "From start to finish, Spain controlled possession. They were the ones looking to play football, create chances and get in behind.

"This is a result for football because of the way that they have played. Spain deserve it."

Spain had 65% possession, an expected goals tally of 1.94 and 20 shots on goal - 11 on target.

At the other end, goalkeeper Unai Simon kept his seventh clean sheet in eight matches as he won the Golden Glove for the tournament's best goalkeeper.

Spain conceded just one goal at the 2026 World Cup - the fewest conceded by any winning team.

Spain are - even in the hard-to-quantify world of international football - clearly the preeminent side in the world.

They are world, Olympic and European champions - not to mention women's world champions too. The men are now unbeaten in their past 38 matches in all competitions - the longest run by any European team in history.

They stumbled out of the gates in this World Cup with a 0-0 draw against Cape Verde - meaning that on both occasions Spain have won the world title they have failed to win their opener, after a 1-0 loss to Switzerland in 2010.

There have only been three instances on record of a player completing 100 or more passes in a World Cup final. Two of those were Spain players on Sunday - Rodri with 101, and Pau Cubarsi with 121.

And for many of the squad, this might just be the start.

Cubarsi, 19, was named young player of the tournament, while it seems very likely his Barcelona team-mate Lamine Yamal has his best years ahead.

At 19 years and six days, Lamine Yamal became the third-youngest player to play in a World Cup final, after Pele in 1958 and Giuseppe Bergomi in 1982. He is now the third-youngest winner.

Sixteen titles in five years - why Spain are a dominant force

Lamine Yamal completes football at 19 as Argentina betray Messi

Trump, Bieber and Shakira - a World Cup final like never before

Argentina are so often painted as the pantomime villains of international football - particularly by England fans.

As they did in the semi-final against the Three Lions, they appeared in the first half to be more interested in stopping any semblance of football occurring than playing the game themselves.

Leandro Paredes was the spoiler in chief, with Enzo Fernandez his deputy.

Though four Argentina players were booked, many will feel that adds further to the theory they have had lenient treatment from officials at this tournament.

Argentina made a total of 464 passes, compared to Spain's 852.

But, unlike England, Spain did not wilt in the second half. Had they won this game long before Torres' 106th-minute strike there could have been few complaints.

Argentina's Emiliano Martinez had to make 11 saves - the most by any goalkeeper on record in a World Cup final.

At the other end, meanwhile, the defending champions became the first team in World Cup history not to have a shot in 90 minutes of a final. Their first attempt came via Lionel Messi in the 117th minute.

Argentina lost their crown, and the scenes that followed the final whistle led many to feel they had lost their dignity too.

Fernandez was first shown a yellow card for dissent, then a second for a spectacularly late challenge that sent Cubarsi somersaulting.

After the full-time whistle, Paredes was involved in scraps with Rodri, Gavi and Borja Iglesias and team mate Nahuel Molina was seen throwing a punch at a passing Spain player too.

"Embarrassing, seeing that sort of stuff after the game has been played," said BBC pundit Wayne Rooney. "Someone has been crowned World Cup champions. It is not good enough.

"Paredes is better than that. It doesn't stop there. He keeps going and going."

Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni attempted to defend his side's performance through floods of tears at his post-match media conference, but could only admit the best team had won.

"Even though they were out-playing us until the 85th minute, I would love to know what would have happened had we still had 11 men on the pitch," he said of Fernandez's dismissal.

"We managed to go all the way to the end when the match was so open and it just couldn't be. They deserved the victory, that is the reality."

Argentina might have taken the World Cup final to extra time - the fifth of the past six to go that far - but that could not mask their inferiority.

"Argentina can have absolutely no complaints about what happened," former England goalkeeper Joe Hart told BBC One. "Disgusting behaviour.

"I don't think Rodri needed to get in their faces but you understand that after 120 minutes where you have been wound up and you thought you've been wronged, and the referee has been on your back."

title: ‘They’d call us animals’ – World Cup final security slammed amid two-hour queues and ‘total chaos’
description: The security outside of the New York New Jersey Stadium ahead of the 2026 World Cup final has been criticized. Supporters attending the match have faced long queues just to enter the stadium before Spain play Argentina at 3pm EDT.
fullText:
The security outside of the New York New Jersey Stadium ahead of the 2026 World Cup final has been criticized.

Supporters attending the match have faced long queues just to enter the stadium before Spain play Argentina at 3pm EDT.

The United States Secret Service are said to have taken over security measures for the game because US president Donald Trump will be attending a match for the first time during this summer’s tournament.

Just a few hours before kick-off, fans and journalists launched fierce criticisms of the operation.

Mariana Spinelli, who works for leading Brazilian outlet Globo, took to social media platform X to write: “We’ve been in line for 2 hours for the x-ray to ENTER the stadium. Everything disorganized, a total chaos. If this were in South America, they’d call us animals, third-world country. Since it’s in the US nobody says anything. A nightmare of organization, pathetic.”

Spinelli is clearly of the belief that if such events were to happen in South America, there would be a much higher level of outrage.

Zach Lowy, the chief editor of Breaking The Lines, wrote: “The organization for this World Cup Final has been shambolic. Thousands who’ve been waiting for 3 hours, there’s a lack of security and officials and scanners, a lot of people who are cutting in line, and a lot of people who have gone around in circles without any progress.”

A further complication also arose when soccer fans were warned to attend the stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey up to four hours before the kick off time. Gates opened at 11am EDT.

A glitch in the FIFA security system to scan passes resulted in long queues forming.

There are believed to be two levels of security at the stadium, one of which is run by the Secret Service and the other organized by the Transportation Security Administration (TSA).

TSA usually oversee airport security. Fans and members of the media attending Sunday’s World Cup final having to pass through airport style scanners before being admitted into the venue.

title: When will the United States host the World Cup again?
description: Fans likely won't have to wait too long to see the World Cup return to the U.S.
fullText:
The United States had to wait 32 years between opportunities to host the men's World Cup. But after the final whistle sounds in Sunday's World Cup Final between Spain and Argentina at MetLife Stadium, U.S. fans shouldn't expect nearly as long of a gap between World Cups stateside.

FIFA's questionable manipulation of the hosting cycles will make that possible.

Basically, FIFA's process for choosing the World Cup hosts goes in a cycle between continental confederations. The 2030 World Cup wasn't just awarded to Spain and Portugal, but Morocco also joined the bid to use Africa's turn in the cycle with Europe. Then, FIFA announced that it would hold opening centennial matches in Argentina, Uruguay and Paraguay. This exhausted CONMEBOL's (South America) turn in the cycle.

While this maneuvering was an obvious effort to award the 2034 World Cup to Saudi Arabia ahead of its usual turn, it also made a quicker return to the U.S. a near certainty.

FIFA has yet to award the 2038 World Cup hosting rights, but fans should expect the tournament to return to the U.S., Mexico and Canada.

Only Oceania and North America would be eligible to host the 2038 World Cup under the current cycle rules. But with Australia competing in the Asian confederation, no viable host exists in Oceania. New Zealand doesn't have the stadium infrastructure.

And given the success of the 2026 World Cup, FIFA is absolutely going to look to cash in on another U.S. World Cup as soon as possible.

Countries eligible to host the 2038 FIFA World Cup 

(by u/-yolewpaniaq) pic.twitter.com/dOEAsoAHAN

— OnlMaps (@onlmaps) July 18, 2026

The 2031 Women's World Cup will also be in North America with Mexico, the United States, Costa Rica and Jamaica hosting the tournament.

This article originally appeared on For The Win: When will the United States host the World Cup again?

title: Donald Trump goes off-script on 'bagpipes' tangent in World Cup Final interview
description: President Donald Trump was interviewed ahead of the FIFA World Cup Final between Argentina and Spain, but the 80-year-old went off-script and began rambling about Scotland and bagpipes during the conversation
fullText:
President Donald Trump went off on a tangent during a pre-match interview ahead of the FIFA World Cup Final.

On July 19, Argentina faces Spain in the Final of the World Cup. The two nations battle for the world championship title at MetLife Stadium, branded as New York/New Jersey Stadium by the tournament, in East Rutherford, New Jersey.

Prior to the match on Fox Sports Live, President Donald Trump, 80, was questioned about how the United States Men's National Team had captured the world's attention, via The Mirror US.

Donald Trump sparks outrage with Harry Kane World Cup comments

Kylian Mbappe drops World Cup truth bomb after absurd third-place loss to England

Instead, Trump veered off-topic and began talking about Scotland — this following his boasts about pressuring FIFA chief Gianni Infantino over USMNT striker Folarin Balogun's red card, which triggered widespread controversy.

TV personality Jenny Taft, 38, posed the question: "It has been so special to see fans all over the world fall in love with our country. How proud does that make you?"

Trump responded: "Well, I watched, and it started with Scotland, and all of a sudden they're doing things that nobody has ever seen with the bagpipes. And it was beautiful.

"And it just went to every country. And we're the hottest country in the world right now," he concluded.

Trump's previous World Cup comments also drew significant backlash. England head coach Thomas Tuchel delivered a sharp rebuke after the President took aim at the team following their World Cup semifinal defeat to Argentina, during which Trump specifically criticized the tactical deployment of captain Harry Kane.

To listen to the latest episode of 'All Out Soccer' - CLICK HERE

"Harry, who's been fantastic, I think they perhaps made a mistake when they made him a defensive player. What do I know about soccer?" Trump initially said.

"They took the lead, and they took their best player and then put him on defense. What do I know about coaching? But that was a little unusual," he closed.

Tuchel was questioned about Trump's remarks ahead of England's third-place match against France on July 18. "Do you use Donald Trump as your witness for the case?" the ex-Chelsea boss responded to the reporter.

England then produced their finest performance of the tournament. England defeated France 6-4 in a thriller.

Following the victory over the French, Tuchel dismissed Trump's criticism and declared, "This game will help us for sure – even if you can never fully celebrate a bronze medal.

"It's the first World Cup medal for 60 years, first at a World Cup on foreign soil, and I hope the players can be proud of that at some point.

"We set ourselves the highest dream to chase, and we were very, very ambitious with our dream to make the final in the World Cup. So it's very, very painful if you miss out; the pain will stay for a while," he finished.

title: World Cup final half-time show: Who is performing, how long will it take and how can I watch it?
description: The finale will feature a half-time show with some of the biggest names in music, including Madonna, Shakira and BTS
fullText:
The World Cup 2026 final is drawing closer with Spain booking their place in Sunday’s match in New Jersey after a fine display to beat France 2-0.

England battle Argentina in tonight’s other semi-final in Atlanta to decide the other finalist.

But Fifa President Gianni Infantino has modernised the tournament with influence from its co-hosts United States and the way some of its biggest sporting events operate, including the Super Bowl in the NFL.

World Cup final live: Spain v Argentina team news and half-time show updates

This will mean a half-time show appealing to more forms of culture and new fans.

Here’s everything you need to know about the Fifa World Cup 2026 final half-time show:

The half-time show will be on Sunday, 19 July 2026 at New York New Jersey Stadium, USA, the location for the final match, with kick-off set at 8pm BST (3pm ET), the half-time show is due to start from 8:50pm BST (3:50pm ET), with stoppage time and additional time to set up the stage and infrastructure perhaps leading to a start time after 9pm BST (4pm ET).

Sources tell the Independent that the show will be around 20 minutes. There is the prospect of the usual 15-minute half-time break, followed by the planned 11-minute show, with Infantino emphasising the figure is inspired by 11 players on the pitch for each team.

Due to the laws of the game, governed by Ifab (the International Football Association Board), it is understood half-time breaks should “not exceed15 minutes,” but Sunday is due to break that law.

There is a precedent set from last year’s Club World Cup final between eventual winners Chelsea and Paris Saint-Germain, which was also organised by Fifa and held in the same venue in New Jersey. That show lasted 24 minutes, with Coldplay, J Balvin, Doja Cat, Tems and Emmanuel Kelly featuring.

The game is on both BBC and ITV in the UK, with ITV revealing that it will broadcast the entire World Cup half-time show at the New York New Jersey Stadium, as well as match analysis.

The BBC will confirm its plans in the coming hours. Alternatively you can follow The Independent’s coverage in our live blog, which will be packed with updates from the show and reaction from social media and our reporters in the stadium.

The 11-minute Super Bowl-style show will be co-headlined by Madonna, Shakira and K-pop boy band BTS.

Justin Bieber was added to the bill, while Burna Boy, Gustavo Dudamel and the PS22 Chorus featuring Coldplay also involved, and Chris Martin has curated the show.

The closing ceremony starts at 18:30 BST (1:30pm ET), with the show including Tom Cruise, Laura Pausini, Nicole Scherzinger, Robbie Williams and IShowSpeed. And Jennifer Hudson is to sing the United States' national anthem.

He was asked: “I’ve got to ask, Argentina-Spain, give us your prediction.”

He said: “Well, I knew you were going to ask, so I thought about it very hard. I hate to get involved even though politically it doesn’t matter too much.

“The head of Argentina is a friend of mine who has done a terrific job, but I would say it’s hard to bet against Messi. I watched that pass (vs England) I think you’d call it a pass. It was exactly perfect.

“It takes some talent to hit the ball off the head coming at that speed, but that was perfect if you had a strike zone, that would be perfect. Just before that, the way he got away from the defender, he was defended very well; all of a sudden, he’s standing by himself, so it’s hard to bet against him.

“I won’t pick sides but I think it’s very hard to bet against Messi; he’s great.”
